D7 Print L5 Evaluate Reference

Moiré as Vector Subtraction

overlay two gratings and a third, much coarser pattern appears — its frequency is simply the difference of the two frequency vectors

Each grating is a frequency vector k — its length is the spatial frequency, its direction the line orientation. Overlaying two gratings multiplies them, and the eye picks out the beat at the difference vector k₁ − k₂. A small angle or frequency mismatch makes that difference vector short, so the moiré period 1/|k₁−k₂| grows huge.

Rotate the second grating and change its frequency. As the two vectors converge, the moiré coarsens dramatically; pull them apart and it tightens away. The diagram tracks k₁, k₂, and their difference.
